title: Carved hazel tent peg
subtitle: Green-wood peg from a coppiced hazel rod
excerpt: A hazel tent peg carved in twenty minutes from a freshly coppiced or bought green-hazel rod. The axe does the rough shaping; the sloyd knife refines the point and the notch. The project teaches green-wood selection, hatchet use, and the axe-to-knife handoff that underlies most green-woodwork.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (c. 1885) for the hatchet-to-knife rough-shaping method and the green-wood selection notes. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) basketry supplement for sourcing and preparation of freshly cut hazel rods. Traditional peg-carving method is public-domain craft practice.

content.text: A tent peg is a good introduction to green-woodwork because it is useful, structurally simple, and teaches the two core skills of the discipline in twenty minutes: how to read a
green-wood
billet

for straightness and length, and how to move from the hatchet to the knife without switching mental mode. The hatchet removes the bulk; the sloyd knife finishes. Hazel (Corylus avellana) is the traditional peg wood: straight-grained, tough, and flexible enough not to split when driven into hard ground. A coppiced hazel rod gives a round cross-section that needs no splitting, the peg is carved directly from the whole rod.

This project uses green hazel, freshly cut or within a few weeks of cutting. Dried hazel is harder and more prone to splitting when driven into stony ground. If you are sourcing from a timber merchant, ask specifically for green or unseasoned hazel rod; dried coppice poles are sold for bean supports and fencing, and are not the same thing.

attrs.body: Default is push cuts, blade away from the body; a dull tool slips more than a sharp one.
attrs.title: Knife direction

content.text: What you need, wood

One green hazel rod, 32 to 35 cm long and 2 to 3 cm in diameter. The rod should be straight for at least 25 cm of its length, minor sweep is fine, but a sharply bowed rod will carve into a peg that sits at an angle in the ground. Hazel rods straighten somewhat as they dry, but a peg carved from a badly bowed rod will always be slightly curved.

How to know the wood is green: the cut end surface will show a slight gloss and the end grain will feel slightly damp to the touch. Chips from a push cut will be slightly waxy rather than dusty. If the rod has been drying for more than a month, the end grain will have lightened to a chalky white and the rod will sound hollow when tapped, still usable, but the point will take longer to carve.

The best source for green hazel is your own coppice plot, or a local coppice worker who sells rod bundles. A hazel stool cut on a seven-to-ten-year rotation produces straight rods of exactly the right diameter. If growing your own hazel for coppice isn't a current project, timber merchants that supply basket-makers carry green hazel rod. See the Garden hazel coppicing guide for the full harvest-to-use cycle.
What you need, tools
Small hatchet

, a two-bevel hatchet with a 35 to 45 cm handle for rough shaping the point. Mora, Hultafors, and Gransfors all make appropriate sizes; a 600 to 900 gram head is sufficient for hazel-rod diameter. The hatchet does not need to be a dedicated carving axe; any balanced general-purpose hatchet works.
Sloyd knife
, for refining the point and cutting the notch. The same knife used for whittling; no additional tools are needed.
Leather strop
, for the knife. Strop before you start and every fifteen minutes. Green hazel contains silica in its cortex that dulls a blade faster than seasoned hardwood.
Chopping block
, a heavy end-grain block, knee-high, that absorbs hatchet blows and holds the billet steady. A thick section of a felled log works; a purpose-made side-axe block is more stable for indoor use.
Step by step
1. Select and measure the rod

Cut the rod to 30 cm. Mark 8 cm from one end, this is the notch line, the junction between the head of the peg (the section that accepts the rope loop or groundsheet eyelet) and the shaft that goes into the ground. The remaining 22 cm will taper to the point. Mark the opposite end for the point so you know at a glance which end is which during carving.
2. Rough the point with the hatchet

Place the rod on the chopping block with the point end overhanging the edge by 5 to 6 cm. The rod is not held during hatchet work, it rests under its own weight. Strike the hatchet at a low angle to the long axis of the rod, taking thin oblique slices rather than straight-across chops. Aim to reduce the end to a rough four-sided taper over the final 12 to 14 cm of length. Rotate the rod a quarter-turn after each pair of strokes so the taper develops evenly on all four sides.

The hatchet is doing roughing work only; the point at this stage is blunt and angular. Do not try to bring the hatchet taper all the way to a fine point, stop when the end is roughly octagonal and 8 to 10 mm across. The knife will take it from there. Remove the rod from the block and check: the point should taper evenly from both the side profile and the end-on view. If one side is higher than the other, put the rod back on the block and take another pass on the high side.
3. Refine the point with the sloyd knife

Switch to the sloyd knife. Hold the billet in the four-finger grip with the point facing away. Using push cuts at 30° to the surface, work the hatchet facets into a smooth taper, travelling toward the tip. Green hazel at this stage will take clean, slightly waxy curls. Strop the knife if you notice the surface going slightly furry rather than slicing cleanly.

Work around all four faces of the rough taper, then round the point proper with thumb-pivot cuts. The finished point does not need to be razor-sharp, a rounded taper of 3 to 4 mm diameter will drive into most ground more reliably than a sharp spike, which deflects off stones. Work the point to a slight taper on all sides. Sight down the length of the peg from the head: the point should sit centred on the rod axis, not canted to one side.
4. Cut the notch

At the 8 cm mark, cut a notch that will hold the rope loop or eyelet. The standard peg notch is a simple V-channel cut across the narrow side of the peg, roughly 5 mm deep and 6 to 8 mm wide. Begin with a stop cut straight across the top of the intended notch, pressing the knife blade vertically to a depth of 5 mm. Then make a second stop cut on the other side of the intended notch. Now make a push cut angled toward the first stop cut from the head end, lifting the chip between the two stop cuts. The result is a clean V-channel.

Test the notch by looping a doubled length of cord over it and pulling sharply downward. The notch should hold the cord without letting it slip up the shaft. If the walls of the notch are too vertical and the cord rides up, deepen the angle of the V slightly with a paring cut.
5. Chamfer the head

The head of the peg (the blunt top end) will receive mallet blows when the peg is driven into the ground. Chamfer the top edge all around with the sloyd knife using push cuts: a 3 to 4 mm bevel around the perimeter prevents the head from splitting or mushrooming under the mallet. This is a thirty-second job, but it extends the useful life of the peg significantly.
6. Dry the peg

Green hazel shrinks as it dries, but a tent peg's dimensions are forgiving; small changes in diameter do not affect function. Leave the peg in a warm, ventilated place for a week or two before use if you want it to be fully stable. For immediate use, green pegs work well: the slight flexibility of green wood can be an advantage in rocky ground. The peg will narrow slightly over its first season as the moisture leaves.
No finish is applied. Hazel tent pegs are outdoor utility items; a finish would add nothing and make the peg harder to clean.
Variations
Ash

, tougher than hazel and slightly denser; the traditional peg wood for clay soils where more driving force is needed. Ash rods are harder to source green in small quantities than hazel. Work the same way.
Split peg
, for a sturdier peg from a larger-diameter rod,
rive
the rod along the grain first to get a half-round or quarter cross-section, then carve the point from the flat face. The flat-backed peg drives more cleanly into compacted ground.
Heavier-gauge peg
, scale the diameter to 3.5 to 4 cm and lengthen to 40 cm for storm pegs in soft ground. The hatchet work takes longer but the method is identical.
Care

Rinse mud off after use and leave to dry standing upright, stored damp, hazel pegs will develop mildew in a stuff-sack. Check the notch each season; a worn notch can be deepened with two stop cuts and a paring cut. A hazel peg will last four to six seasons with reasonable care; when the point is too worn to drive cleanly, split the old peg for kindling and carve a new one.
What can go wrong

items.fix: Keep hatchet strikes oblique, the blade angle to the rod axis should be less than 45°. If a knot is present, work around it with the knife rather than trying to chop through it. Knot sections are tough enough to deflect the hatchet safely but will fight a straight chop.
items.cause: The hatchet is being driven too steeply into the end-grain, or the rod has a short-grain section where a knot was.
items.symptom: The hatchet splits the rod lengthways rather than slicing it

items.fix: Sight down the peg from the head end and identify the high side. Take push cuts on the high side with the knife until the point sits centred. This is easier to correct at the rough-taper stage than after the point is fully formed.
items.cause: More material was removed from one face than the other during hatchet roughing.
items.symptom: The point comes out offset to one side

items.fix: A clean notch always starts with a definite stop cut. If the existing notch is crumbled, pare it back to clean wood and re-cut from the stop cut inward. Strop the knife before the notch work, a sharp blade cuts a clean-walled notch; a dull blade tears.
items.cause: The notch was cut too quickly without a proper stop cut first, or the knife was dull.
items.symptom: The notch walls crumble under the cord

items.fix: Chamfer the head. If the peg has a lengthways check crack, retire it, a cracked peg will split along the crack under impact. For hard ground, pre-drive a hole with a thin steel pin to reduce the force needed.
items.cause: The head was not chamfered, concentrating the mallet impact on the end-grain rim; or the peg dried too quickly and has a check crack running lengthways.
items.symptom: The peg splits across the grain when driven into hard ground
